summary: Market sentiment turned cautious as Wall Street closed lower to kick off Q3, with tech and chip stocks — which had led record rallies in Q2 — pulling back sharply, while 2026 laggards provided some offset in an otherwise mixed session. On the macro front, U.S. factory activity eased from a four-year high with input prices remaining elevated, soft jobs data lifted gold more than 2%, and Goldman Sachs estimates the 2026 World Cup could artificially boost the June jobs report by roughly 40,000 positions, complicating the Fed's read on labor market strength. Geopolitically, defense stocks in both the U.S. and UK gained momentum — supported by a $20 billion UK spending boost and defense startups sourcing parts from auto and fracking sectors — while Iran-related headlines dominated, with U.S.-Iran talks in Doha centering on the Strait of Hormuz, Iran insisting on retaining control over the waterway, and senior analysts warning a conflict could materialize sooner than markets anticipate. The primary risk factors investors are monitoring include Middle East escalation threatening oil supply routes, sticky input inflation constraining Fed rate-cut timelines, Bitcoin's summer weakness creating crypto-sector uncertainty, and soaring new vehicle prices signaling persistent consumer cost pressures. The short-term outlook remains cautious, with geopolitical risk premiums likely to stay elevated, energy prices anchored near $72–$75 per barrel per Petrobras guidance, and any further deterioration in U.S.-Iran relations or hawkish Fed signals capable of extending the early-Q3 selloff in risk assets.
